item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see this was called out when the re-write was new, but the typo is still there so I want to call attention to it again.

In the first letter, he says he is killing people who are less happy than she is. But context and the next letter make it clear this is supposed to be victims more happy than her.
It's possible this is a purposeful mistake, showing that the scientist is. y'know, not quite right in his thinking.
But it really feels like a typo, and one that distracted me pretty bad when I was reading.

Also, we have a special page for -N. It should get more attention, and moving this good story there might help with that. It would also appeal to my incipient OCD sense of a tidy mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in reply to this:

from the SCP Note B-01:

[…] And so I asked myself: how could I make you happier? The answer: by removing those from the Earth that are less happy than you are. Surely if the net amount of joyful people decreases, the standard for happiness will decrease as well, no? […]

asking permission to replace the word marked in red with the word 'more'.

it's more than just fixing a typo and i am not sure if this might not be intended by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It started off as something different until I rewrote it when it was in the negatives.

Speaking of which, this isn't my best work…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the comments make me think these people were reading something different D: this is creepy enough for my upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a lot better! Have this upboat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooh, Me like. Clever, a little creepy, and very derranged. I makes me want to know more, but a little scared to find out. Nice work!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"LIKE I'M MAKING SAUSAGE!"

I saw the comment before I read the re-write. I read it in his voice.

Upvoted for reasons not related to the aforementioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As an aside, the man is partially inspired by Ladd Russo from Baccano.

item.title: Re: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful. What an impressive change! I thought of ways the original idea could work, but you turned this around and how!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Neat concept, well executed. I like that the crazy thinks hes sending them to his girl, but he's sending them into the Hudson River where bodies end up anyway.

In my mind, she's dead already and he's trying to send them to her, somehow, and he's using magic but he thinks its science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I print articles all the time with collapsables. Is there a big group that converts to PDFs that I don't know about?

I like collapsibles, feels like supplemental materia" in a manila folder or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … shouldn't he be removing those that are HAPPIER than her? that way, there's a lot of happyness to redistribute and she's closer to the top… taking out the lower steps only puts her lower, right? o.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then you removed the vibe you had going in the first iteration that I did actually somewhat like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, but the content wasn't as good as the hook, so it kinda didn't feel right.
Still good enough for an upvote though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's irritating to read the old containment procedure if they're all crossed out like that.

Hm. Well, I sort of like the new narrative than the old one, of an insane lovesick scientist:

Surely if the net amount of joyful people decreases, the standard for happiness will decrease as well, no?

Though its simpler, I was impacted by the story more than before the rewrite. You also addressed my question, I'll give it a +1.
One final gripe though:

Due to their length, they are extremely small and require several orders of magnification to read properly

How would she be able to read it?

item.title: Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
Extensive rewrite and altering of core concept based on feedback given.

Skip name changed to "Dearest Caroline".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[REDACTED] requiring further study as to their capabilities.

What could this possibly pertain to? I hardly ever complain about redaction, but I didn't feel like this added anything to the article.

You're definitely on to something here. My advice is: don't explain everything. The what's-going-on is laid out nicely for us in these perfectly preserved notes, leaving almost nothing to the imagination (aside from "How in the hell did those get in their mouths when the corpses disappear?") This would benefit from a lot of confounding hints. Also, I thought the symbol was fine, but the "element 130" and all the pseudo-science sounded hokey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to give it a +1 because I didn't find anything wrong with this article. I'm curious to learn more about what they were trying to accomplish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The notes are in a chronological order, yet they (the GoI) just spotted the teleportation effect after few instances? And why did they put some sensitive information into the bodies of the subjects sent no one knows where? It would be fine if the perpetrators were just crazy, but the described GoI has a clear, legible modus operandi and sealing discriminatory notes inside test subjects seems… extremely strange at this point.

In my opinion, the article explains too much and risks becoming mundane. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd usually avoid voting on an article rated 0 but I really can't see why people are having a problem with this one. It's not amazing but certainly not bad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with everything Sal just said. This article seems to me like pretty good example of why GoI's don't automatically make something interesting, except that the GoI here is a one-shot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something interesting here, it peeks in and then just falls completely flat.

As a specific nitpick: if we've never been able to record a manifestation of SCP-1925-A, how do we know the two in the addendum manifested simultaneously?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letters are not up to snuff although the set up is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I am the one that started the collapsible hate, and he has simply repeated my points.

  • Collapsibles break flow and tone.
  • Collapsibles prevent pages from being printed properly.
  • Collapsibles prevent pages from being converted into PDFs.

If it's only a few lines, a collapsible isn't necessary in the first place.

If it's long enough to merit a collapsible, a supplementary page would exert no more stress than it does now (seeing as there's no images or anything else to bloat the site's footprint), reduce the load on initial render, and be easier to index.

*slapfights in Ihpkmn's general direction*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is trying too hard. The only bit I liked was the lead-in, where I was excited that this would be a great article about some kind of weird serial killing. Instead you went straight to the "interesting bit" and stretched it out for such a long time. For an SCP with such an intriguing start-up, I'd really love to see a pay-off instead of just a meh Entity of Interest that eats up the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about: if we didn't have collapsibles, almost every page on the wiki that does would have a supplemental page, sometimes consisting of only a couple of lines, and then the wiki's servers would melt and the Foundation would go bye-bye.

*stops before a staff post*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same, except I'm downvoting.

It also doesn't say anything about whether or not any of the bodies have been identified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if I'm missing something beyond "attempting to do bad stuff to kids but made them teleport", but I'm not enthralled here. Also not sure which elements are video game easter eggs and which aren't, which makes it difficult to read. Neutrality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about: collapsibles break several features of site automation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with the whole 'collapsibles break the flow' mentality that's been going around the site lately. I just think of them as accessing a separate document and bringing it up at the same time you're reading the document it's attached to. In printed-out form, it would probably just be attached to the main document with a paperclip or stapled to the back of it.

Besides, can you think of a better way to present parts of the document that are only accessible if you're a certain clearance level?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit too contrived for me, but I couldn't say it would work better as a story.

As far as I comprehend, this just presents to us a time/dimensional travel trope and does nothing else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image is hokey, and I dunno, it feels a little sparse, a little like it's trying very hard to say something without saying it outright. You revealed too much of the backstory right there in the notes, but never really bring it to a satisfying conclusion. Mmmmm… but I like it, and it's good enough for an upvote.

E: Also, collapsibles break flow and mess with printing and archiving. If you could take that out, that'd be great, okay?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has there been study put in to discover the cause of death for each corpse?
Other than relying on the "Element 130" notes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Image Source)

Thanks to anqxyr and VincentRedgrave for their help.

I'm not 100% sure about it, but I've polished it fairly well and feel as if I've done what I can with the premise. Hope you like it.

Footnote: Aspects of this were inspired by the video game Singularity, so there are a few easter eggs in there.
